sentence: Subjective:
The patient is a 56-year-old male who presents with a complaint of difficulty swallowing that started approximately 4-5 weeks ago.
He reports that he feels like something is stuck in his throat, which is painful when he swallows solid foods, and he has to wash it down occasionally.
He has not experienced any coughing, vomiting blood, or weight loss.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of diabetes and high blood pressure.
He is currently taking metformin 500mg twice a day.
His hemoglobin A1c was last checked 7.4.
He has been following his diet and exercise regimen and reports that his blood sugar levels have been within the target range.
He has also been taking norvasc, and his blood pressure is currently 120/80.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea.
He has not noticed any blood in his stool or any changes in his bowel movements.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes; Norvasc for high blood pressure.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 120/80.
Physical exam:
The patient's neck is fine, with no swelling or masses.
The thyroid gland feels normal, and there are no lymph nodes.
The throat exam is normal, with no masses or swelling.
The lung exam is clear, and the heart exam is normal.
The belly exam reveals some epigastric tenderness, but no masses or significant swelling are felt.
The back exam is normal, and the extremity exam is fine.
The neurological exam is normal.
Test Results:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has difficulty swallowing, suspected to be caused by a possible narrowing of the esophagus, possibly a stricture.
The patient also has diabetes and high blood pressure.
Problem list: Difficulty swallowing (suspected stricture) active; Diabetes (controlled) active; Hypertension (controlled) active.
Plan:
I recommended that the patient undergo a gastroenterology consultation for further evaluation and possible dilation of the esophagus.
I prescribed Prilosec 20mg once a day for gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) and advised the patient to chew their food thoroughly, especially hard foods like steak or meat.
I also recommended that the patient follow up in one month for a repeat hemoglobin A1c test and to check the results of the blood work ordered today.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient in one month to review the results of the blood work and hemoglobin A1c test.
The patient will also be seen in gastroenterology for further evaluation of their difficulty swallowing.
